name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Applied Physics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The five-year plan builds logically along three well-ordered spines: CS (CS 100 -> 113 -> 114 -> 241/280 -> 341/350/435 -> 490/491), physics (PHYS 111 -> 121 -> 234 -> 335 -> 430/432 -> 442/485), and math (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 211 -> 222/335), with a deepest prereq chain of 7 confirming genuine term-over-term progression. Integration points such as CS 301 Data Science, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling, and OPSE 310 Virtual Instrumentation sensibly tie the two disciplines together, though the first year is heavily front-loaded at 18 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a curriculum map showing how the physics track feeds the computing courses (e.g., PHYS 485 modeling drawing on CS 301/CS 435) so the dual-degree rationale is explicit rather than implied by co-scheduling.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible with a deepest chain of 7, reasonable for a rigorous 5-year dual degree, and grade conditions are clean throughout. The 7 dangling references (missingCourseCount 7: CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A, GER 200, GER 300) are all lab co-requisites or GER category placeholders rather than broken CS/physics chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the lab co-requisites (CHEM 125A/126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A) and the GER 200/300 category codes so these dangling references resolve and missingCourseCount drops toward zero.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a plan of study and course list are present. This is a real catalog finding, and it matters more here because a dual degree needs an explicit statement of the integrated computing-plus-physics competency it produces.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes covering computing foundations, applied-physics modeling, and integrated computational problem-solving directly on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta 0.0; summedTermCredits 134 = statedTotalCredits 134), and the distribution spans a heavy STEM core (CS, physics, math, chemistry) with writing, ethics (IS 350), and GER woven in. Load is uneven, however: the first year is 18 credits while the two final terms drop to 12, and 134 credits is light for a true BS/BS pairing, worth confirming.
dimensions.suggestion: Level the term loads (redistribute from the 18-credit first year) and annotate the gen-ed vs. major-core vs. elective credit split so the balance is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled with per-term credits shown, but the page carries PDF-extraction artifacts: 'or MATH 328 or Mathematical Methods...' and 'or R750 404 or Quantum Mechanics' alternatives appear as separate credit-less rows, and a stray '232 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences' line bleeds in from an adjacent program.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study parse so 'or ...' alternatives fold into their parent course and the stray '232 ...' cross-program fragment is removed.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course set is strong for scientific-computing careers: CS 301 (data science), CS 351 (cybersecurity), CS 356 (networks), CS 435 (algorithms), CS 490 (software engineering) on the computing side, and PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling of Applied Physics Problems, and OPSE 310 Virtual Instrumentation on the physics side. The main gaps are a dedicated ML/AI course and any explicit high-performance/parallel computing for large-scale simulation.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a machine-learning-for-science elective and an HPC/GPU computing option so graduates can apply modern compute to the physical-modeling work PHYS 485 introduces.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present, specific, and informative, naming concrete topics (CS 341 on automata/computability/complexity, CS 435 on balanced trees/hashing/graph algorithms, MATH 335 on Gauss/Green/Stokes theorems, PHYS 485 on computer modeling). None read as thin placeholders; only excerpts are truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with consistent grade conditions ('with a grade C or better') and sensible alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114'), and intro courses correctly show none. Clarity is only modestly reduced by references to lab co-reqs and GER categories that are not themselves catalogued.

missingTopics.rationale: Computational physics depends on numerical analysis, yet MATH 328 (Mathematical Methods) is only an 'or' option and no dedicated numerical-methods course is required; make a numerical-methods course a required bridge feeding PHYS 485.
PHYS 485 Computer Modeling and OPSE 310 imply large simulations but the plan has no GPU/parallel/HPC course; add one so students can run realistic physics workloads.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning for the physical sciences
missingTopics.rationale: CS 301 introduces data science but there is no ML course applied to scientific data or surrogate modeling, now central to computational physics; add a scientific-ML elective.
Neither the CS nor physics track includes visualization or large scientific-dataset analysis, a core skill for experimental and simulation work; add a data-visualization module or course.
With PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics plus a strong CS theory core (CS 241/CS 341), the program is unusually positioned to offer quantum computing but currently does not; add a quantum-computing elective.

emergingTrends.rationale: The rare combination of required quantum mechanics (PHYS 442) and CS theory makes this dual degree ideally placed to lead on quantum algorithms as the field industrializes.
Physics-informed neural networks and ML surrogates are transforming simulation; CS 301 plus PHYS 485 are natural anchors for a scientific-ML pathway.
Modern applied-physics modeling runs on accelerators and HPC clusters, so an HPC skill set would directly extend PHYS 485's computer-modeling work.
Coupling OPSE 310 instrumentation with simulation points toward digital-twin engineering, a growing area in aerospace, energy, and manufacturing.
Automated, ML-guided experimentation is reshaping physics labs; the program's instrumentation and computing base could position graduates for this niche.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes for the integrated CS-plus-physics competency (currently none are present).
Require a numerical-methods/scientific-computing course and add an HPC/parallel-computing option to properly support PHYS 485 computer modeling.
Add a scientific-machine-learning elective and, leveraging PHYS 442, a quantum-computing elective to occupy the program's distinctive niche.
Level the uneven term loads (18-credit first year vs. 12-credit final terms) and annotate the gen-ed/major-core/elective credit split.
Clean the plan-of-study artifacts (fold 'or MATH 328' / 'or R750 404' alternatives into their parent rows, remove the stray '232 ...' line) and register the 7 dangling lab/GER references.
